title: Intro to Generative AI
subtitle: Machines That Create — Text, Images, Music and More!
overview: From ChatGPT writing essays to DALL·E painting pictures to Suno composing songs — Generative AI is reshaping the world at breathtaking speed. In this session, students peel back the curtain on how generative AI actually works: what it means to 'generate', how large language models predict the next word, what training data is, and why these systems can be brilliant yet sometimes wrong. Students also explore the ethics, opportunities, and responsibilities that come with this powerful technology.

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — AI-generated image examples, LLM diagrams, student prompt experiments, whiteboard sketches.

keyTopics: Generative AI — AI systems that create new content (text, images, audio, video, code) rather than just classifying or predicting
Large Language Model (LLM) — A neural network trained on massive text data that generates human-like text (e.g. GPT-4, Gemini, Claude)
Training Data — The enormous dataset of text, images, or audio an AI model learns patterns from during training
Parameters — The billions of adjustable numbers inside an LLM that encode learned knowledge (GPT-4 has ~1.7 trillion parameters)
Next-Token Prediction — How LLMs work: given the previous words, predict the most probable next word/token, repeat billions of times
Transformer Architecture — The neural network design (introduced in 2017) that powers all modern LLMs using attention mechanisms
Prompt — The input you give to a generative AI model; better prompts produce better, more relevant outputs
Prompt Engineering — The skill of crafting effective, detailed prompts to get the best results from an AI model
Hallucination — When an AI generates confident-sounding but factually incorrect or entirely made-up information
Diffusion Model — The technology behind AI image generators (DALL·E, Stable Diffusion) that starts with noise and refines it into an image
Bias in AI — Unfair or skewed outputs caused by patterns in biased training data reflecting real-world inequalities
Ethical Use of AI — Responsible AI use: avoiding plagiarism, not spreading misinformation, respecting copyright, and understanding AI limitations

activity.title: Prompt Engineering Challenge + AI Fact-Check Lab
parts: Computer or tablet with internet access
Access to ChatGPT, Gemini, or any LLM (free tier)
Access to an AI image generator (DALL·E, Canva AI, or Adobe Firefly)
steps: Part 1 — Weak vs Strong Prompts: Give students a weak prompt ('Write a story') and a strong prompt ('Write a 100-word adventure story about a 12-year-old girl in Mumbai who discovers her grandmother's old robot'). Compare the outputs — which is better and why?

Part 2 — Prompt Engineering Challenge: Each student gets a challenge card (write a poem, explain photosynthesis to a 6-year-old, create a quiz, summarise a topic). They must craft the best possible prompt and share results with the class
Part 3 — Image Generation: Use an AI image tool to generate an image from a text prompt. Experiment with adding style words (watercolour, photorealistic, cartoon). Discuss how diffusion models work

Part 4 — Hallucination Hunt: Ask the AI a question with a false premise or a very specific factual question. Record the answer. Then verify it using a reliable source. Did the AI get it right or hallucinate?
Part 5 — Ethics Discussion: As a class, discuss — Is using AI to write your homework OK? Who owns AI-generated art? What happens if AI is trained on biased data?
Challenge: Write a system prompt that turns the AI into a helpful tutor for a specific subject — then test it with 3 questions and evaluate how well it performs

quiz.q: What makes Generative AI different from traditional AI?
options: Generative AI only works on smartphones
Generative AI creates new content like text, images, and audio, while traditional AI mainly classifies or predicts from existing data
Traditional AI is faster than Generative AI
Generative AI requires no training data
quiz.explanation: Traditional AI classifies or predicts — 'Is this email spam?' or 'What number is in this image?'. Generative AI CREATES — a new essay, a new image, a new song. It generates content that didn't exist before!

options: Logical Language Mechanism
Large Learning Module
Large Language Model
Linear Logic Machine
quiz.explanation: LLM = Large Language Model! These are massive neural networks trained on enormous amounts of text. Examples include GPT-4 (OpenAI), Gemini (Google), Claude (Anthropic), and Llama (Meta).

quiz.q: How does an LLM generate its response — what is its core mechanism?
options: It searches the internet for the best matching answer
It looks up answers in a pre-stored database
It predicts the most probable next word (token) over and over until the response is complete
It copies text from its training data and pastes it together
quiz.explanation: Next-token prediction! Given all the words so far, the LLM predicts the single most likely next word, adds it, then predicts the next one again — billions of times. The result feels natural because the training taught it what words tend to follow others.

quiz.q: What is Training Data in the context of AI?
options: The exercises students do to learn about AI
The hardware used to run AI models
The massive collection of text, images, or other content that an AI model learns patterns from during training
The test questions given to an AI to check its performance
quiz.explanation: Training data is the AI's school! LLMs are trained on hundreds of billions of words from the internet, books, Wikipedia, code, and more. The model learns the patterns in this data and uses them to generate new content.

quiz.q: What is a Parameter in an LLM?
options: A type of prompt given to the AI
One of billions of adjustable numbers inside the model that store learned knowledge from training
The maximum word limit for an AI response
A safety filter that blocks harmful content
quiz.explanation: Parameters are the AI's 'memory'! During training, these billions of numbers are gradually adjusted to encode patterns from the training data. GPT-4 is estimated to have around 1.7 trillion parameters — that's how much it can 'remember'!

quiz.q: Which neural network architecture made modern LLMs possible, introduced in the 2017 paper 'Attention Is All You Need'?
options: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)
Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)
Transformer
Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)
quiz.explanation: The Transformer! Introduced by Google researchers in 2017, it uses 'attention mechanisms' to understand relationships between words across long distances in text. GPT, Claude, Gemini — they all use Transformer architecture.

quiz.q: What is a Prompt when using a Generative AI tool?
options: A type of error message from the AI
The training data fed into the model
The input instruction or question you give to the AI to get a response
The output generated by the AI
quiz.explanation: A prompt is your instruction to the AI! 'Write a poem about monsoon in Mumbai' is a prompt. The quality of your prompt directly affects the quality of the output — which is why prompt engineering is such a valuable skill.

options: Building the hardware that runs AI models
The skill of crafting clear, detailed, and effective prompts to get the best possible results from an AI
Programming the AI model from scratch
Engineering a robot that responds to voice prompts
quiz.explanation: Prompt engineering is the art of talking to AI effectively! Specifying the audience ('explain to a 10-year-old'), format ('in bullet points'), tone ('professional'), and context ('you are a science teacher') all dramatically improve AI outputs.

options: When an AI generates colourful or artistic images
When an AI confidently generates false, made-up, or inaccurate information as if it were true
When the AI's response is too long
When the AI refuses to answer a question
quiz.explanation: Hallucination is a critical AI limitation! LLMs sometimes generate plausible-sounding but completely made-up facts — fake book titles, wrong dates, non-existent people. Always verify important AI-generated information with reliable sources!

options: Because they are connected to unreliable websites
Because they are designed to always give an answer even when uncertain, completing patterns even when the correct answer was not in the training data
Because their training data was too small
Because they don't have enough processing power
quiz.explanation: LLMs are pattern-completion machines — they always try to produce a plausible next token. When they don't 'know' something, they fill in the gap with what statistically seems likely rather than saying 'I don't know'. This produces confident but wrong answers.

quiz.q: How do AI image generators like DALL·E create images from text prompts?
options: They search Google Images and combine existing photos
They use a Diffusion Model — starting from random noise and gradually refining it into a matching image
They draw pixel by pixel using if-else rules
They find the closest image in their training data and send it back
quiz.explanation: Diffusion Models! They start with a completely noisy image (random pixels) and gradually 'denoise' it step by step, guided by the text prompt, until a clear image emerges. Each step makes the image more aligned with your description.

quiz.q: What is Bias in AI, and where does it come from?
options: A bug in the AI's programming code
When the AI charges more for some users
Unfair or skewed outputs caused by patterns of inequality or prejudice present in the training data
When the AI takes longer to respond to some questions
quiz.explanation: AI learns from human-created data — and human data reflects human biases. If training data has more examples of doctors being male, the AI may generate male doctors more often. Bias in → Bias out. Fixing bias requires diverse, representative data and careful evaluation.

quiz.q: Which of these is an example of a Large Language Model?
options: Google Maps
Microsoft Excel
ChatGPT (GPT-4)
Spotify
quiz.explanation: ChatGPT uses GPT-4 — a Large Language Model made by OpenAI. Other LLMs include Google's Gemini, Anthropic's Claude, and Meta's Llama. These are all trained on massive text datasets to understand and generate human language.

quiz.q: A student uses ChatGPT to write their entire science assignment and submits it as their own work. What is the key ethical concern?
options: The assignment might be too long
ChatGPT uses too much electricity
It is academic dishonesty — the student is presenting AI-generated work as their own, not developing their own understanding
There is no concern — AI tools are always allowed
quiz.explanation: Using AI to do your work without disclosure is dishonest — it bypasses the learning process and misrepresents your own ability. Ethical AI use means using it as a tool to help you learn and improve, not to replace your own thinking.

quiz.q: What is the best way to handle information generated by an AI like ChatGPT?
options: Always trust it completely — AI is never wrong
Never use AI-generated information under any circumstances
Treat it as a useful starting point but verify important facts using reliable and authoritative sources
Only use it for creative writing, never for factual topics
quiz.explanation: Critical thinking is essential with AI! Use AI to explore, brainstorm, and draft — but verify facts, check sources, and apply your own judgement. AI is a powerful tool, not an infallible oracle. The human stays in the loop!
